reasoning:
The main driver for this question was the 3.1σ anomaly in the R_K measurement reported by LHCb in March 2021. However, in December 2022, the LHCb collaboration released an updated analysis with more data and an improved understanding of backgounds. The update showed that the measurements of R_K and R_K* are completely consistent with the Standard Model, thereby resolving the anomaly and effectively ruling out the primary evidence for lepton universality violation. Other anomalies, like R(D) and R(D^*), remain at much lower combined significances (around 3σ) and have not sharply increased. 

Furthermore, the knowledge cutoff is October 2025, leaving only about two months until the resolution date (end of 2025). The process of a major physics collaboration observing a 5σ anomaly, announcing it, writing a paper, submitting it, and having it pass peer-review takes many months. Because no such 5σ violation has been announced as of late 2025, it is practically impossible for a peer-reviewed paper making this claim to be published before the end of the year. Therefore, the probability is set to the lowest supported value.
